Okay, okay.

Because I'm in Rhode Island.

I'm based in Dallas.

Yeah, I'm not a spider person, for sure.

not a good idea.
You're yourself drama there, for sure.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, thank you so much for joining.
I'm really excited to chat, and thanks for your patience.
It sounds like you were waiting a little bit to chat.
I can give you a little bit of a sense of the role, how I'm thinking about it.
Who did you chat with before you talked to me?
So I talked with Tucker, and then...
I'm drawing a blank on her name.
Bridget?
Bridget, correct.
Okay, cool.
Bridget's my manager.
So she probably gave you a great overview, but I can just talk to you a little bit more about what we're thinking about.
And then from there, it will be your time to shine.
And then I'll give you 10 minutes at the end to ask questions.
Very standard interview process.
Cool.
So GrowthX, you're probably aware we're growing very fast.
And we think of ourselves as a sort of deployment forward marketing agency.
We operate under sort of a services agency model, but we do have an underlying product that we use on the back end to generate all of our content.
We're just generating like blog content, newsletters, things like that.
We don't do like paid or we do some videos, but it's not our bread and butter.
So, like, SEO content strategy is really, like, the core of what we do.
And our AI-driven platform, like, generates all of that content.
So as a services model, we operate in pods.
So we have, like, a content manager at the base layer who's actually interacting with the AI platform.
We call it Atlas Day-to-Day.
They're generating the content.
A managing editor who manages, like, three to five accounts is kind of the daily project manager.
We're producing five-ish articles a week for every client.
And so that person is, like, literally editing the content.
We're very big on human in the loop for all of the content that we generate.
And then just, like, making sure that our deliverables are on track on a week-to-week basis.
And then the account strategist is kind of an overarching...
account strategist thing.
thing.
strategist strategist
Role that oversees two to three managing editors in pot in those pods.
And so you'll have like 10 ish accounts, I think that number will fluctuate, hopefully, as we gain leverage.
And with that, you're kind of thinking long term, owning the relationship, thinking about like strategy, you know, we're signing year long engagements with clients.
And so from a marketing perspective, obviously, marketing plan is not going to stay the same over a year, there's a lot of touch points.
And like evolution of the strategy with a client over the course of time.
So the account strategist is the person that like drives that like long term engagement and makes sure that we're kind of on track for renewal, expansion, things like that.
This isn't like a, you don't like carry a quota at this point.
I don't know what that will look like in the future.
what like the future.
But it's really about engagement, thinking of it.
think initially we were thinking of it or kind of selling it as a fractional CMO.
I don't know if it's to that level, but that's the aspirational aspect of it.
So there's a lot of room to drive strategy for some really impressive and exciting companies that we're working with.
But it's some of that CS and account management background, which is like I have just a core CS background.
I don't have any marketing background.
But we do want somebody that has some of that like marketing and SEO experience to be able to like think strategically about the approach that we're taking to.
That makes sense.
So my takeaway is this role will essentially be having the person leading the relationship with the accounts, making sure that engagements are.
And on track that you're connected deeply with the right folks to be on top of the strategy to continue to drive, you know, the results that we're all looking for.
So that makes sense.
Yeah.
Much better and more succinct way of putting it.
But I had to give you all the context.
The context is helpful.
I had some great combos thus far.
And I think everyone's perspective is painting a more full picture.
And it's super appreciated.
Perfect.
Awesome.
Well, I'd love to learn more about you.
Of course.
So I'll give you the full story.
So I started my career actually in hospitality.
And my North Star has always been making sure people are having the best experience.
I could be a mixture of people-pleasing in the past, but that's always sort of been my North Star.
So I was in hospitality for quite some time.
And I worked in operation.
So looking out for efficiencies, making things better long-term.
I took a small break to become a yoga teacher.
I
Super random.
And so I'm going to Atlanta and did that for a bit of time.
And then I got into this company called WeWork.
And WeWork was essentially my, like, entry point to understanding many different types of industries from people who do sales, people who do marketing, all the things.
And just through building relationships in that role, I was introduced to a company called Service Titan, which was my first job as a customer success manager.
What I found to be really interesting at Service Titan is this is the place where I gain thick skin, right?
You're working with people in the trades, plumbers who don't like technology.
And so you're just taking it to the chin and trying to drive results for these business owners.
What I noticed at Service Titan is that there were a lot of things that folks were doing that were repetitive and I thought could be done in a more effective, scalable way.
And so I started to get my hand into skilled success.
And so as I was getting into that, I was connected with someone at Airtable who reached out and said, hey, we're looking for someone to be our first CSM.
For our emerging small business team, we eventually want this person to sort of lead skilled programs and one-to-many motions for the business.
Did that for a few years and ended my time there as a program manager for Skilled Success.

So highly working with customers one-to-one to make sure that they are getting what they need from us, helping them with adoption, ROI, and then also building out things like community, webinars, et cetera, to educate customers on the product.
And fast forward to today, I'm working at a company called WRITER, which is an AI company.
We work with companies of all sizes, primarily enterprises, and I'm in a hybrid role.
And what that means is I'm working with a subset of accounts, more like strategic accounts, if you will, to help them with using WRITER.
We are a one-stop shop for building agents, using generative content, you name it, WRITER can do it.
And so what I love about that is that it's constantly changing and that I'm learning something new every single day.
I'm working with people in different...
From marketing, to operations, to retail, medical.
And so I have a plethora of experience working with these different verticals and making sure that they are getting the most value from WRITER.
And in tandem to that, also working on skilled programs.
So webinars, how can customers help each other, peer-to-peer support, and really driving that sort of muscle within the business.
As we see now that customers need a lot of one-to-one support, and it's super important to also figure out how to do this at scale in order to grow sustainably.
So a little rundown of my current experience and where I'm at today.
Yeah, awesome.
Super helpful.

Talk to me, I'm like really curious about the scaled program, like your scaled work, which seems to be the core of your experience, which is amazing because we're thinking a lot about how we scale right now.
Um, so how did you balance kind of, especially at the beginning of scaling, um, and like the strategy there, how do you think about, um...
So like balancing an increasing number of accounts with clients that might be more used to like higher touch points or how do you think about scaling in that way?
That's a great question.
The truth is scale is still fairly new.
It's only been a buzzword for the last few years.
Yeah.
I do equate a lot of my success and skills having a good leader in the past who really taught me and showed me the way.
But I think it's about getting buy-in and everyone knows that the problem is that we need to figure out how to scale.
But the most important thing is understanding prioritization.
When you do decide to lean into a more scaled motion, you have to be okay that there's also going to be some downside and a great amount of upside as well, right?
And so it's understanding, okay, who are our ideal customer profiles?
Who do we want to focus on?
And make sure that we're giving that one, that one, two, one, touch too.
And then who are those that we still care about and value?
However, we understand that to see long-term growth, we have to put our efforts into this particular profile.
And so...
so...
so...
That is a conversation that takes collaboration, buy-in from sales, buy-in from leadership, to be aligned, to make sure that you are working towards that common goal.
And then most importantly, being super connected to the customers.
I think at the end of the day, it's about what they need.
Obviously, we're here to guide them and to give our recommendation, but ultimately understanding what is it that they want to see.
Maybe it's sending out a survey of what would be interesting for you to attend.
Is it a webinar around SEO and changes that we're seeing in the market?
Or is it a community where you can work with other folks in your same vertical to understand how they're doing things, right?
And so there's a lot of room for experimentation and creativity.
And I think the most important thing to step into a space to do scale is having the understanding across the board that it is experimental, that we will sometimes mess up.
We will sometimes get it right.
But as long as we're keeping the customer front and center of what we're doing, the long-term results are going to be perfect.
Awesome.
So can you give me an example?
can give me an

Maybe you have a time when you like either in your scaled work or another time when you had to work cross-functionally, you talk a little bit about buy-in, would love to like learn about your process there.
Oh my goodness.
I think the team that I typically most work closest with the most are folks in sales.
And one of the biggest challenges I think at any tech company is that sales is going to sell, right?
They're going to offer the world.
And so level setting with customers and with your peers is just crucial.
I think what I found to be most helpful is not saying no, but redirecting to an alternative that is beneficial for all parties.
So for example, a salesperson might say, you know, you have unlimited trainings for your team, right?
And then I might come in and say, we do offer trainings.
Here is a live event that we're hosting.
It'll be very beneficial for you.
And then after, let's meet for maybe 30 minutes, answer any questions one-to-one.
That way you are, you know, rotting a larger group to the bulk of what you want.
I them to learn, but then also having that one-to-one touch step so that it feels personal.
And then from there, you can change the narrative to whatever you see fit, right?
At that point, I'm owning the relationship.
I can educate customers on what makes the most sense for our engagement and partnership together.
And then from there, showing the success of it in sales is now bought into how that can work in a way that's beneficial for everyone across the board.
Awesome.
Cool.
Cool.
So talk to me a little bit about your, I don't, I have like, I've heard of WRITER, but I don't really know what they do.
So like, talk to me about your day-to-day work there.
And, you know, part of this is like thinking about marketing strategy.
And I know that's some of what WRITER offers.
So would love to learn more.
I tell people that I have a new job every two weeks.
And what I mean by that is that like, the product's constantly changing, the trends in AI are constantly changing.
So there's a lot of, a lot of noise.
And so sometimes good, sometimes bad, but the.
So job is constantly changing.
So if I were to, again, I'd have a hybrid world.
focusing on programs and then focusing on customers, I would say for the time that I'm dealing with customers, a lot of it is making sure that they're onboarded successfully and using the product.

And so when I'm measuring success at WRITER, it's not just about did you log in, but it's about tying the things that you're using to valuable use cases that are going to either save you time or money long term.
So first and foremost, always understanding what are the top priorities for the customer.
So aligning on what teams are we working with?
What work are they doing?
What does this cost you today?
How long does it take you today?
And then using different reporting metrics to be able to compare and contrast the evolution from point A to point B.
In there, our product, we have like chat agents, similar to like chat GPT.
You're able to build your own agents from scratch using like chain prompting and different no code.
Right.
No code experience, if you will.
And then there's also the piece of like using agentic workflows as well.
So like full stop, like you start it and then it ends all with agents.
And so just the whole, the whole works, if you will.
And so it just depends on the week.
Sometimes there's an emphasis on AI studio is what we call it to build your own agents.
Sometimes there's a push on using Ask Writer, which is our chat agent.
And so it really does change.
know that's kind of like a broad answer.
I would say in the conversations that I'm having, it's getting people to understand that it's more than just like writing emails, right?
It's, you can do really meaningful things like content, SEO improvements, and just walking them through like what other customers have done and how they can bring that benefit to their team as well.
yeah.
Awesome.
Um, you kind of answered this, but I'll just like ask it more specifically.
So how do you structure?
For your work week, like when juggling those multiple deadlines, you talked a little bit about prioritization, would love to know a little bit about your like organization.
I'm working on not being so type A, Andi, I'm working on it.
And it's really challenging.
My personal life to work, everything is on a calendar, like lunch breaks, gym workouts.
So everything is organized in that way for me.

And the reason why I do that is, A, it helps me remember what I've been working on, but also I'm able to really optimize my time from a layer of prioritization, from doing this for quite some time, you understand when someone is, you know, the person who cried wolf versus an actual urgent emergency, right?
If someone says, Oh, this isn't working.
Okay, like I knowing their use cases and what they're using the product for, I have that discernment to know, like, does this require me to immediately connect to the customer?
Or is there a support article or something that I've built in the past that I can send them to help them resolve what they're currently doing?
Another medium that I love to use.

With my customer specifically is Loom or any sort of video recording tool for folks who do like that personalized touch and need to be walked through it because I can do that within, you know, three minutes, right?
It saves me 30 minutes of meeting on a call, but also getting them what they need in a quick way.

So just thinking through like tooling and ways to just work more efficiently helped me also with prioritizing where I can plug and play and put time for certain projects versus where I actually need to like be one-to-one with a client.
Yeah.
Sorry, I'm just taking notes.
I'm not getting it, no worries.
Cool.
Can you tell me about a time when a client deliverable was going off track and what you did to help improve it?
Yes.
So it's ironic because the customer just like starts screaming at me in my head.
So I'm working with this account dial path.
And, obviously, this is two-fold, right?

So we have a speed that we want to go as a company, but there's also the element of you have to also go the speed that works for the customer, because not everyone is as AI-forward as an AI company is going to be.
And so, a timeline that we have internally for getting someone onboarded is 30 to 60 days.
We want them to have their users in, everything configurated from style guide, and they're team trained.
And, however, the reality is, folks also have other priorities.
And, again, sometimes adoption is a little bit slower for other companies.
And so, what comes to mind for this particular account is that they were a bit behind on when we would want them to have everyone into the product and using it.
And I had to chat with our buyer to understand, like, what is going on here?
Like, we have been reaching out to them.
We've been wanting to get these things on the books.
However, things just weren't happening.
And I had to really get to know this person.

Buyer and understand like what's top of mind for them and aligning those priorities to how we can help them long term to get him to see, okay, we have to do this now and this is urgent and this is important.

So I think that relationship of like deeply understanding what they're working on, their company culture, what are their deadlines and mapping them to our roadmap, if you will, of onboarding to have a product time or project timeline that works for both parties.
Now, the caveat is that even though I knew that, the work was educating my team internally on this is what was happening.
And so something that I tend to do is I am, I mentioned type A, like note taking is my thing.
I'm a firm believer in like having super active like Slack channels for each customer that I work with.
And my goal is to always provide a weekly update in those channels so that anyone can have visibility into what's going on.
I think when those notes aren't there, it leaves more room for question, which then.
It distracts you from the work that you're supposed to be doing.
So I try to be proactive in my communication so that I can spend time on the things that matter for the customers that I'm working with.
So I think to wrap it all up, I think it's having deep relationships, being communicative internally and externally as well to make sure that everyone that's involved is aligned.
I think that kind of ties into like the program management experience, the CS experience, and that's how I've been able to be successful in my work at WRITER and beyond.
Yeah.
Awesome.
I want to come back to the work at WRITER.
Are you guys like helping clients at all think about their marketing strategy or anything?
Or are you really helping them?
I mean, obviously your goal is like product usage, but to what extent are they coming to you with like marketing questions writ large?
Yeah.
So it's more so product usage.
Obviously, every company.
It has their own marketing strategy, and there are different stages of their evolution with that.
Something that I do do is keep them in the loop on trends that are happening in the marketing industry.
So I'm not making the decision of, hey, this is how you have to do your marketing, but hey, here's a suggestion on what other people are doing that might be helpful.

And I think being at WRITER, we are not only looked at as like the AI experts, but they're coming to us because they expect us to have a level of understanding of the work that they're doing as well from just like a general perspective.

Since we do work with like different companies and different teams, whether it's like support or sales or marketing content, there is this idea of being, having enough knowledge to be dangerous, but not necessarily to narrowly focus on one thing that you're not able to help customers that might need something else.
So it's dangerous, knowing enough to be dangerous, I think with...
It sounds like you guys have a couple of different product lines.
And also that you need to kind of like just be aware of what's happening in the general marketing world.
So like, how do you stay up to date on things?
For me, it's just naturally being curious and also being a fan of AI myself in general, like I use it personally, right?
And so I think once you have that like personal buy in of like, actually like this software as a solution, or I like this product, if you will, you're able to take it to market better.
So I think, one, being bought into the vision is super important.
But from an education perspective, we have a really strong muscle of enablement at WRITER.
And it's also expected for us to be in close partnership with our product team.
I am basically the voice of the customer.
I'm working with them directly.
I'm receiving their feedback.
Customers also do a great job of keeping us informed as well, because they are looking at.
Competition, they're seeing things from their network and bringing it to us to bring to our product because they want to see it in the platform as well.
And with that, have to be able to articulate what their need is.
So if someone shows me a new software that just launched, in order for me to articulate what it is to our product team, I have to take the time to actually figure out, okay, what is this thing doing?
And honestly, I can use AI to help me do that.
I literally love generative AI.
It's super helpful across the board.
And so I think the strong enablement muscle from our internal team, getting feedback from customers and having to work with product, I'm bringing that to life.
And then just general curiosity, because I'm interested in AI, it really helps me be dangerous in the field.
Cool.
Awesome.
Well, those are all the questions I had.
So what questions do you have for me?
Andy, I think one of the biggest things that are top of mind for me is just knowing that there's a lot of things moving in the AI market, right?
There's my question for you is when you.
So the company, what about it made you think this makes sense for not only my career, but for the folks that you all are marketing to and supporting?
Like, what was that aha for you personally coming into a smaller startup company?
Yeah, so I think the piece about GrowthX that's so different from what everybody else is doing in AI is that we are a services company at our, like, first right now.
And so that means that it's not, we're building a product and it has to, like, it's usage-based, and if people aren't using it, they're going to churn.
We're delivering, like, content, the content itself, and so we're operating today in an agency model.
think long-term, we are not a marketing agency, but it does create a lot of space in terms of profit.
For us to be like, our clients are paying us $10,000 a month.
And our product is like only internal clients don't have access to our product right now, right?
So it's like that human in the loop is I think a differentiator in terms of like what people are selling at AI.
We're very real about like, everything's built on AI, but AI is not like completing the loop, right?
Like, AI cannot get you 100% of the way they are on content today.
I mean, you probably are not allowed to say that, but I can say it.
No, it's absolutely true.
It's like, I mean, similar at Riot of the philosophy, there should always be a human in the loop.
One thing that I also want that just comes to mind is one of the biggest challenges with AI adoption is time.
And so I think what you all are doing that's really smart is offering the service versus the product.
Because one of the biggest delays to adoption is, we know we
We to do it.
We know we have to learn it, but we don't have the people to do it.

So you buy this heavy ticket product, and then you're struggling to show ROI because you don't have the resources internally, which is why you'll see in the market, a lot of these AI companies are switching to service models, right?
Okay, well, we have the product that you can use, but now you have to buy this package of service hours, which then increases the ticket item even more.
Yeah.
So what was interesting to me about what you're all doing is similarly is that you're offering it as a service.
And I think, I mean, maybe long term, there is a vision where the product does become available to the market, but right now I think it's...
It's not soon.
There's some time for it.
It'll be a while.
Yeah, there's some time for it.
I mean, like it does like very cool things, but it's not like pretty enough for clients yet.
So I think, yeah, that like services piece is huge and it just like means that we can kind of sign on to client, like have clients sign on and...
Not be worried.
mean, like, obviously, we would love more funding, and like, we're always open to that, but we also aren't, like, our runway is not a concern in the same way.
Now, if my timeline is correct in just understanding the journey of the company, there'll be a lot of renewals coming up in the very near future.
Yes.
I'm ready for renewals, doc, today.
How are you thinking about renewals?
What do you think will be some of the reasonings for churn, if at all, that customers might bring up that I've been working with you for the last year?

Yeah, I mean, I think, like, agency relationships are something that I don't have a lot of understanding or, like, experience with, but I don't know how, like, long-term agency relationships are today.
So, like, some companies hire a marketing agency to, like, get one thing done, and I think we're shifting a little.
A bit of, like, what to expect from an agency and from GrowthX.

Like, we are continually shifting strategy and we're delivering strategy, whereas some agencies are, like, executing on strategy or executing on a strategy that somebody else created or creating a one-time strategy.
So, like, I think the evolution of, like, long-term relationships is, like, an open question of what we're offering.
I mean, we have, we already have some successful renewals where clients are, like, you are, like, you are our marketing team for organic content, which is great.
We, I think, like, it's, it's about the, like, what we can show in terms of evolution and continued improvement, right?
Because, like, you don't just want to be more of the same year over year.
But I think.
We're able to do that.
The other thing that we're working on right now is like better reporting.
So we have some very basic reporting.
think every company has that problem.
But like we have, we can like do basic reporting on like clicks, impressions, things like that.
But to be able to like tell a deeper story about like the tail, the long tail of our content and customer journeys is going to go a long way for us down the line.
And I think what I'm, what I'm hearing is, you know, you mentioned earlier, the fractional CMO as like an example of the kind of relationship that you want to have with your customers.
And I know that you mentioned that the number of accounts could potentially fluctuate from 10.
What would you imagine like the highest number potentially being for one individual person to be able to build that depth of a connection and partnership with these customers for long term success?
I mean, honestly, I don't see don't a...
So managing more than 15 to 20.
That's just like the max probably for the next like year or two.
But I also like that a lot of that will depend on like what the product does and when we end up offering a product solution too.
Cause like right now you're talking to clients on like a weekly or biweekly basis.
So like there's, there's a limit to your time, but you are working with teams that are like delivering regularly.
So like there's a lot that can change, but I wouldn't see in the next year it being more than like 15 or 20.
Got it, got it.
Earlier we talked a bit about prioritization.
Curious to know, is there like an ideal like customer profile that you're thinking of that makes the most sense?
And just how do you view prioritizing?
Uh, the accounts that currently work with you all.
Yeah, that's a great question.
And I think we, uh, we do have an ICP.

What is hard today is that Marcel, our CEO, like, is the person, a lot of the, because he was a CMO at Service Titan and other companies, um, had, like, a lot of our first clients, which are the ones that are, like, renewing right now, are, like, like, Marcel's personal friends or, like, colleagues and things like that.
And so it's, like, well, you're not in our ICP, but, like, you and Marcel had a drink the other day.
So what are we supposed to do about that?
Yeah.
but we're, we're getting better.
Like, we're just thinking about messaging right now and how we, like, help clients understand, too, that, like, we're not the best fit and there are other agencies out there.
Like, we're happy to give business to.
Another agency that can do the thing that they want us to do, you know, like, because demand is not our problem.
We're booked out for like six weeks.
As I'm like hearing this, and it seems like there's gonna, there's like a deeper level of intimacy.
A lot of these accounts know the CEO.
And this is, in my opinion, a really good time to like think of how do you build a stronger network of community within your client base, right?
Rather, it's just like group strategy sessions, right?
Like, that could be something really interesting to explore.
Yeah.
Especially if, you know, there is that level of deepness and personal touch that folks have with these accounts.
Sometimes it's a bit harder when you're with bigger companies, because there's tons of customers who kind of been just flying under the radar, right?
And so it's really interesting.
I think that it's, you go out in a good position for sure.
Yeah.
And I...
So we are building a community.
Jason, our head of growth is building the community.
And we also have a lot like Marcel is really good.
He's done a lot of like breakfasts and networking dinners and things like that.
And they're not pitches.
They're just like, they're almost like VC meal, you know, dinners or things like that.
So there is a lot of like that connection that's happening.
Jason, deal, deal.

If you were to say this is the mission statement or three attributes that I would want my team to have, what would those be?
Um, I think the first thing that I want people to feel is
